questions.question: What shape does the embedding stage produce from (B, T) ids?
options: (B, T)
(B, V)
(B, T, D) where D is the model dimension
(B, T, V) where V is the vocabulary size
questions.explanation: Each id is mapped to a D-dimensional vector. The batch keeps B and T and gains a new feature dimension D.

questions.question: How are token and positional embeddings composed in this lesson?
options: By concatenation along the feature axis
By elementwise sum, broadcasting the position vector across the batch
By an extra linear projection
By taking the elementwise maximum
questions.explanation: The two tensors of shape (B, T, D) and (T, D) are summed. Broadcasting replicates the positional table across the batch axis.

questions.question: How many learnable parameters does a SinusoidalPositionalEmbedding contribute?
options: max_context_length
max_context_length * d_model
d_model
Zero
questions.explanation: The sinusoidal table is computed from a formula and stored as a buffer. It contributes no parameters.

questions.question: Which positional encoding cannot accept a sequence longer than its construction limit?
options: Sinusoidal positional embedding
Learned positional embedding
Both
Neither
questions.explanation: In this lesson implementation, both classes enforce max_context_length at forward time. Learned is inherently table-bounded; sinusoidal here is also bounded because it uses a prebuilt table.

questions.question: Why does the sinusoidal scheme use both sin and cos on adjacent feature pairs?
options: Because sin alone is not periodic
Because cos by itself cannot be backpropagated through
So that the vector at position p+k is a linear function of the vector at position p
Because it doubles the parameter count
questions.explanation: Pairing sin and cos at the same wavelength means a position shift by k is a rotation, which is a linear map. Attention can then learn relative offsets through linear projections.

questions.question: Why does the embedding backward pass only update rows of the token table that appeared in the batch?
options: PyTorch only stores gradients for non-zero entries
Rows not touched in the forward pass produce zero gradient by the chain rule
The optimizer skips rows it has not seen
Token ids are stored in a sparse data structure
questions.explanation: A row that did not contribute to any output has zero derivative for the loss, so its gradient is zero for that step.
